segments.text: Hello everyone, thank you for joining us today. It's a pleasure to welcome you to our session
from IoT to intelligent digital twins integrating APS, AI and auto-destination for real-time
building operations.
Hello, my name is Natalya Barros, I am architect at Santo Tecnica.
I am Eluisa Lvergy, I am also an architect and urban planning here at Santo Tecnica Engineering.
We are both from Brazil.
In this presentation, we will begin with an introduction to the key challenges and concepts
of digital twins.
Next, we will provide a step-by-step guide on how to create the digital twin system architecture.
We will demonstrate how to set up a digital twin and configure it at dashboard in auto-desk
standing to visualize real-time data such as AI-powered people counting and environmental
comfort metrics.
After that, we will show how to use auto-desk standing connect to send automated notifications,
via Microsoft Teams.
Finally, we will discuss the key benefits and strategic impacts of digital twins.
We will start by introducing the key points.
One of today's key challenges is maintaining comfort in work spaces.
Usually, the occupants only report issues once this comfort sets in, prompting a reactive
call to the operational team.
At the same time, operations team often lack a clear understanding of how spaces are
actually used.
Without real-time data, decisions end up based on guesswork.
This is where digital twins come in.
They provide visibility, enable data-driven decision-making based on real behavior, rather
than assumptions.
These are challenges that many professionals face every day.
These challenges prevent us from gaining clear visibility into what's really happening
with the asset.
After all, what we do is, we do not have a clear understanding of the problem.
What we can't measure, we can't manage.
Digital twins provide us with vision, memory and prediction, all within a single platform.
A digital twin consists of three core components.
First, the physical environment, second, the virtual environment, and third, the data flow
that connects them.
These two spaces are linked through a bidirectional connection that keeps them synchronized and
enables the change of control data between the physical and digital worlds.
We implemented our digital twin at TechLab, SONDO Technicas, Technology and Innovation Hub.
Let's now leave the Autodesk Dev, continue and travel to downtown São Paulo, Brazil.
A neighborhood rich in history, culture and architecture, with buildings that reflect
the city's legacy.
TechLab is more than just an oft-off-seafield with desks and computers.
It is a creative space where we explore new technologies and conduct experiments.
Additionally, TechLab serves as a venue for presentations and workshops, welcoming clients
and fostering innovation.
It also includes four smaller rooms designed to board and management meetings.
In other words, TechLab functions as both a workplace and an innovation laboratory, allowing
ideas to move quickly from concept to practice.
We present the digital twin system implementation.
First, we capture the physical environment using MatterPort.
A tool is consistent that combines images with spatial sensing.
This generates a navigable virtual environment, precise spatial measurements and a point
cloud representation of the as-built conditions.
Then the point cloud was clean, optimized and classified to identify architectural and
s-t elements.
Finally, we develop it, being model from the point cloud, modeling our buildings, elements
and adding s-t attributes and semantic information.
Second, we collect data related to indoor environmental quality and s-t management.
To do this, we start with a nice sensor, temperature and humidity sensor, energy consumption
sensor and cameras through half the environment.
The scientific information packets in JSO contain fields such as ID, time stamp, temperature,
humidity, among others to IoT gateway.
This gateway is responsible for locally processing the data.
It's built with a Raspberry Pi board.
In a digit, two cameras were started in strategic spots in the office to count people in real-time.
The AI model use it is JOLO-12.
The system records the height's number of people detected and calculates the average
confidence level for the detection.
The results include the camera ID, time stamp, location, number of people detected,
AI model and method use it.
And are sent to gateway.
After saving the data, the IoT gateway sends the data via HTTP request to the API, hosted
on Microsoft Azure.
A TypeScript code developed by Sondotecnica receives, process and stars the sensor data.
Each sensor camera is identified by two main keys that facilitate organization and visualization
in other desktop and in the database.
A main key address which ensures the unique hardware identification and a unique name to
identify the device.
The payloads then follows two simultaneous flows.
In the first, the data is stored in the post-screal database within Azure.
A second, it sends you to Autodesk Tunding, linking it to the tech lab office digit out
way.
In the Autodesk Tunding, we link the IoT data to being model, creating the digital twin,
where we visualize the real-time data.
Each object within the being model comes with the data information such as materials, dimensions
and inventory numbers.
The digital twin displays real-time data collected from IoT sensors.
Each balance represents an IoT sensor.
Additionally, the platform can showcase the hit maps and charts, which is added to analyze
and monitor the environment.
You can see the dashboard to field the being model, real-time data, compare temperature and humidity
across different areas and assess the data for improving monitoring and durability.
And finally, we show you how to configure it to Autodesk Tunding Connect to send the
automatic notifications via Microsoft's Tings.
To connect Tunding to Tings, we use the two five plugins in Autodesk Tunding Connect.
The first is the scheduled data string, which runs the pipeline every two minutes.
The second is the Autodesk Tunding service plugin, which connects directly to the digital
twin. We use the option retrieve the string-time
serial data to bring it in the sensory history and after that, we use it to convert.
The first converter filters the data, keeping only the last five in-mune to subject temperature
ratings. A second converter sets the other two routes, like the maximum temperature and the
message that you will be sent. Finally, the Microsoft's Tings plugin sends the
other two messages to a channel, which is especially the webihook you want to
use from Tings to Tunding Connect and the other two are sent automatically.
After configuring everything, you can receive the other two rightfully in Tings and also see
temperature differences in Tunding and on the dashboard. This allows real-time visualization
of the collection data. So, when a sensor detects a temperature available above normal,
the change appears in the digital twin and on the dashboard.
An alert message is sent directly to Tings. In short, the architecture of the digital
twin system consists of four layers. The perception layer focuses on data collection,
including activities, sensors, and communication components. The data layer involves cloud
storage, which holds the BIM model and the data collected from the sensors.
The services layer impresses analysis services, where artificial intelligence
algorithms process and analyze the data. Finally, the application layer provides a real-time
in-data visualization within the other SDTundays environments and sends notifications via Microsoft's
strings, Pro or SDConnect. To conclude, I would like to highlight five key points. First,
the necessity of a multidisciplinary team, building an intelligent digital twin isn't just
technical challenge. It requires collaboration between architects, software developers,
data engineers, and operations professionals. Integration only works when diverse expertise
come together. Second, this is a low cost and scalable solution, by using IoT sensors,
edge processing, and Autodesk platform service, we built a robust architecture without
heavy infrastructure investments. Tinteligence comes from smart integration, not from expensive
hardware. Third, the solution enables data-driven decision-making. Instead of relying on assumptions,
facility managers can act based on life, environmental, and occupancy data directly connected to
the digital twin. Fourth, real-time notifications enable rapid response.
Through ultimate workloads, alert triggers immediately when thresholds are exceeded,
reducing response time and preventing operational issues before they escalate.
Fifth, this is just the beginning. The next step is expanding monitoring to incorporate
additional variables such as energy consumption, water usage, and predictive analysis analytics.
Moving toward a more autonomous and intelligent building ecosystem. Ultimately,
the digital twin is no longer just a model. It becomes a powerful decision support system.
I'd like to thank this amazing and highly skilled team that supported us in building the digital
twin. Our team includes a big specialist assisting analysts, a process data specialist,
and IIT and AI-speciting. So thank you so much for our attention. Thank you.
